A flexible developing move. While this move usually leads to a Queen's Gambit, it is also used to go into a Colle System—a solid line that prepares e2-e4 earlier and can be more aggressive than the usual lines.
d5 is the classical approach to fighting for the centre: occupying it with pawns. d4 takes some space for Black and opens a line of development for Black's queenside bishop.
d4 is the Queen's pawn opening. d4 White looks to take over control of the centre.

The Austrian chess players Hans Haberditz (c. 1901–57), Hans Müller (1896–1971), and GM Ernst Grünfeld studied this defense, which was initially introduced by Italian chess player Alessandro Salvio in 1604.

Arising from the Austrian Defense, the Gusev Countergambit, named after the Soviet Master of Sports Nikolai Nikolaevich Gusev, is represented by the gambit of a pawn on c5 with 3. In distinction to traditional openings that prioritize immediate control, Black elects to relinquish central pawns for active piece play.
The a logical and principled way of dealing with this central tension, using a flank pawn to exchange a center pawn. Black usually replies with 3.

The Queen's Indian Defence is a popular and fairly modern opening whereby black quickly develops his queenside bishop, either by fianchettoing it on b7 to contest the long diagonal, or on a6 to put pressure on white's c-pawn. It has been employed regularly by a number of top players including former world champions like  Anatoly Karpov and Viswanathan Anand.
Nf3 develops a piece and contributes to White's fight for the center of the board. This move strengthens the d4 and e5 squares and brings White a move closer to castling kingside.
e6 is a move with several purposes. First, it opens a diagonal for the development of Black's dark-squared bishop.
White takes more space and increases the control of the important d5 square. This move order allows White's queenside knight to develop to the active c3 square without blocking the c-pawn.
Black controls e4 while developing a knight. This is the most flexible response to 1.
d4 is the Queen's pawn opening. d4 White looks to take over control of the centre.

annotation.moveRationale: Be6 develops a piece to an active square in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
Rc1 repositions the rook toward an open or key file in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
exd5 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
Nxd5 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
Qxe7 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
Bxe7 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
Nxd5 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
cxd5 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
b6 prepares the fianchetto, aiming to place the bishop on a long diagonal in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
e3 reinforces the center solidly, maintaining flexibility in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
Castles kingside, securing the king and connecting the rooks in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
Bh4 develops a piece to an active square in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
h6 is a prophylactic move, preventing an opponent's piece from reaching an active square in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
Bg5 develops a piece to an active square in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
Nf6 develops a piece to an active square in the Queen's Gambit Declined.
Nf3 develops a piece to an active square in the Queen's Gambit Declined.

Black stops White from playing the immediate 4. Bg5, practically forcing White into one of three options:

/4.

Nc3, White continues development and puts pressure on the d5 pawn, and also controls the e4 square for a possible e4 break in the future. White wants to play e4 at some point in the future, and the move 3.
e6, Black declines the Queen's Gambit in the traditional fashion. e6 bolsters the d5 pawn and opens a diagonal for the development of Black's dark-squared bishop.
The Queen's gambit is one of the oldest openings in chess. White offers a pawn in order to gain control of the centre.
d5 is the classical approach to fighting for the centre: occupying it with pawns. d4 takes some space for Black and opens a line of development for Black's queenside bishop.
d4 is the Queen's pawn opening. d4 White looks to take over control of the centre.
